transcript.speaker: Alice Chen (PM)
transcript.text: Alright, let's get started. We've got Sprint 14 kicking off tomorrow and I want to lock down the scope for the Notification Preferences API today. Bob, Carol, thanks for jumping on.

transcript.speaker: Bob Rivera (Eng)
transcript.text: Yeah, happy to. I've been looking at the rough spec you shared — the CRUD endpoints for user notification preferences. I think we can get a solid v1 out this sprint if we keep it tight.

transcript.speaker: Carol Wu (Design)
transcript.text: Same here. I've got the settings panel mocks ready, but I need to know what the API shape looks like before I finalize the toggle states.

transcript.speaker: Alice Chen (PM)
transcript.text: Perfect. So let's walk through it. At the core, we need standard CRUD — create a preference, read it back, update it, delete it. Each preference ties to a user ID and a notification channel like email, SMS, or push.

transcript.speaker: Bob Rivera (Eng)
transcript.text: Makes sense. I'm thinking we go with Express and TypeScript for this. We've been standardizing on that across services anyway. And for testing, Vitest — it's faster than Jest and the DX is better.

transcript.speaker: Alice Chen (PM)
transcript.text: Works for me. Carol, any concerns on the tech side?

transcript.speaker: Carol Wu (Design)
transcript.text: No, that's fine. I just care about the response shape. Can we make sure the GET response includes an enabled boolean and the channel type? My toggles map one-to-one to those.

transcript.speaker: Bob Rivera (Eng)
transcript.text: Absolutely. I'm thinking the preference object looks something like — id, userId, channel, enabled, createdAt, updatedAt. Clean and flat.

transcript.speaker: Alice Chen (PM)
transcript.text: Good. Now there's one more thing from the product side. We need a batch update endpoint. The settings page lets users toggle multiple channels at once, so we need to support updating up to 500 preferences in a single request.

transcript.speaker: Bob Rivera (Eng)
transcript.text: Five hundred? That's — I mean, okay, but are we actually expecting anyone to hit that limit?

transcript.speaker: Alice Chen (PM)
transcript.text: Probably not day one, but enterprise accounts could have hundreds of notification types. I'd rather set the contract now than change it later.

transcript.speaker: Bob Rivera (Eng)
transcript.text: Fair enough. I'll cap it at 500 and return a 400 if someone tries to go over. The batch endpoint can be a PATCH to /preferences/batch with an array of partial updates.

transcript.speaker: Carol Wu (Design)
transcript.text: That works for the frontend. We'll just collect all the dirty toggles and send them in one shot.

transcript.speaker: Alice Chen (PM)
transcript.text: Great. Now the other big piece — webhooks. When a user changes their preferences, downstream services need to know. Think email service, push notification gateway, that kind of thing.

transcript.speaker: Bob Rivera (Eng)
transcript.text: Okay, so we're talking about firing a webhook on every preference change? Do we need delivery guarantees here? Like, a retry queue or—

transcript.speaker: Alice Chen (PM)
transcript.text: No. Let's keep it fire-and-forget for v1. We post to the registered webhook URL and move on. If it fails, it fails. We can add retry logic in v2.

transcript.speaker: Bob Rivera (Eng)
transcript.text: That simplifies things a lot actually. I'll just do an async HTTP POST after the preference write completes. No queue, no dead letter, just send and forget.

transcript.speaker: Carol Wu (Design)
transcript.text: Quick question — will the webhook payload include the full preference object or just the ID?

transcript.speaker: Bob Rivera (Eng)
transcript.text: I'd say full object. It's small enough and it saves the consumer from making a callback to fetch it.

transcript.speaker: Alice Chen (PM)
transcript.text: Agreed. Full object in the webhook payload. Now let's talk about what we're not doing this sprint, because I know scope creep is going to come up.

transcript.speaker: Bob Rivera (Eng)
transcript.text: Oh, I already have a list. No auth layer, right? We're assuming that's handled upstream by the API gateway.

transcript.speaker: Alice Chen (PM)
transcript.text: Correct. No auth. No database either — we're doing in-memory storage for v1. Bob, you mentioned a repository pattern?

transcript.speaker: Bob Rivera (Eng)
transcript.text: Yeah, I want to use a repository pattern so we can swap in Postgres or DynamoDB later without touching the route handlers. For now it's just a Map in memory, but the interface stays the same.

transcript.speaker: Carol Wu (Design)
transcript.text: Does that mean data resets every time the server restarts?

transcript.speaker: Bob Rivera (Eng)
transcript.text: Yep. That's fine for dev and testing. We'll persist for real in the next sprint.

transcript.speaker: Alice Chen (PM)
transcript.text: And just to be explicit — no retry queue for webhooks, no WebSocket support, no notification categories or grouping. Those are all v2 or later.

transcript.speaker: Bob Rivera (Eng)
transcript.text: Someone's going to ask about WebSockets, I guarantee it.

transcript.speaker: Alice Chen (PM)
transcript.text: And we'll point them to the roadmap. For now, REST plus webhooks covers the use case. Carol, anything from the design side we're missing?

transcript.speaker: Carol Wu (Design)
transcript.text: I think we're good. As long as the API returns consistent shapes and the batch endpoint works, I can build the settings UI against it. One thing — can we make sure error responses also have a consistent format? Like a standard error object with a message and code.

transcript.speaker: Bob Rivera (Eng)
transcript.text: Yeah, I'll set up a global error handler middleware. Every error response will have the same shape — status, message, and an optional details array for validation errors.

transcript.speaker: Alice Chen (PM)
transcript.text: Alright, I think we've got a clean scope. Let me recap: CRUD endpoints for preferences, batch update up to 500, fire-and-forget webhooks, Express plus TypeScript plus Vitest, in-memory storage with repository pattern, and explicit no-gos on auth, database, retry queue, WebSockets, and categories. Everyone aligned?

transcript.speaker: Bob Rivera (Eng)
transcript.text: Aligned. I'll have the project scaffolded by end of day and the CRUD endpoints up by Wednesday.

transcript.speaker: Carol Wu (Design)
transcript.text: Aligned on my end too. I'll finalize the settings panel mocks to match the API shape Bob described. Let's sync again Thursday to check progress.
